executiveSummary: QA Test Academy projects $749,127,660 in Year 5 revenue with a 98.1% profit margin. The model tells a strong financial story with 10 of 10 key metrics in healthy range, a great foundation for your mission.
biggestRisk: No facility location secured yet, so facility costs are estimated

recommendations.title: Plan for Tuition Collection & Discount Risk
recommendations.description: Private schools face collection risk from late payments, withdrawals, and financial aid shortfalls. With 99.9% of revenue from tuition, build a 5–10% bad debt reserve into your budget. Factor in tuition discount rates for merit/need-based aid and maintain clear enrollment contracts with payment terms.

recommendations.title: Curriculum & Instructional Costs Are High
recommendations.description: Instructional program costs average $45,000 per student. Most schools target $500–$2,000 per student for curriculum, supplies, and assessments. Review whether premium curriculum is justified by your educational model and outcomes.

recommendations.title: Secure Your Facility Location
recommendations.description: Your model is based on an estimated facility budget. Once you have a signed lease or purchase agreement, update your model with actual numbers because real lease terms make your projections far more reliable.

lenderReadinessExplanation: This model demonstrates strong financial fundamentals: a clear path to profit, controlled costs, a sustainable revenue mix, and adequate debt coverage. Your projections are grounded and achievable.

keyMetrics.interpretation: Healthy per-student revenue provides a solid foundation for sustainability.

keyMetrics.interpretation: Total cost per student is $67,195, well within revenue per student of $1,080,756, leaving a healthy margin.

keyMetrics.interpretation: Operating costs are lean relative to revenue.

keyMetrics.interpretation: Year 1 shows a healthy surplus, a strong start for a new school.

keyMetrics.name: Profit margin (Year 5)
keyMetrics.interpretation: By Year 5 the model shows strong profit, and your school is on track for financial sustainability.

keyMetrics.interpretation: Strong projected revenue growth over the 5-year period.

keyMetrics.interpretation: Year 5 enrollment approaches facility capacity, an efficient use of space.

keyMetrics.interpretation: By Year 5, the school has built a healthy operating reserve of 3+ months, a strong foundation for long-term stability.

enrollmentGuidance: Year 1 to Year 2 projects 39% enrollment growth. Growth over 25% in a single year is uncommon and may require aggressive marketing or facility expansion.
Year 2 to Year 3 projects 28% enrollment growth. Growth over 25% in a single year is uncommon and may require aggressive marketing or facility expansion.
Year 5 enrollment of 230 exceeds facility capacity of 200. You'll need a larger facility or phased admissions.

topIssues.title: Enrollment growth needs demand evidence
topIssues.summary: Year 1 to Year 2 projects 39% enrollment growth, and enrollment exceeds your facility capacity of 200 students.
topIssues.whyItMatters: Demand is the engine of your financial model. Every revenue line, staffing decision, and cost assumption depends on filling seats. If enrollment falls short, the entire model breaks downstream.
topIssues.recommendedAction: Back every enrollment target with documented demand — signed letters of intent, waitlist depth, community survey results, or recruitment pipeline data. Growth over 25% per year typically requires an exceptional recruitment engine or facility expansion.

topIssues.title: Staffing costs growing faster than enrollment
topIssues.summary: In Year 2, staffing cost growth outpaces enrollment growth by more than 10 percentage points.
topIssues.whyItMatters: Hiring ahead of demand locks in fixed costs before the revenue to support them arrives. If enrollment doesn't materialize as planned, staffing becomes an unsustainable burden.
topIssues.recommendedAction: Align staffing additions to enrollment milestones. Hire when students are enrolled, not when you hope they will be. Use part-time or contract roles to bridge gaps.

healthSignals.label: Healthy
healthSignals.explanation: The model reaches profit early and sustains strong margins through Year 5.
healthSignals.watchItem: Monitor enrollment actuals vs. projections in Year 1.

healthSignals.label: Healthy
healthSignals.explanation: Cash stays positive throughout the projection and reserves exceed 3 months of expenses.
healthSignals.watchItem: Maintain reserves as a buffer against seasonal revenue dips.

healthSignals.label: Healthy
healthSignals.explanation: Staffing at 1% of revenue leaves room for facilities, programs, and reserves.
healthSignals.watchItem: As you add staff, ensure staffing stays below 60% of revenue.

healthSignals.label: Healthy
healthSignals.explanation: Facility costs at 1% of revenue are well-managed and leave budget for programs.
healthSignals.watchItem: Watch for rent escalation clauses that could push this higher over time.

healthSignals.label: Healthy
healthSignals.explanation: Revenue is anchored to enrollment-driven income — the strongest foundation for a school model. A focused revenue stream is a strength when backed by dependable demand.
healthSignals.watchItem: Continue to pressure-test enrollment assumptions: recruitment pipeline, waitlist depth, retention rates, and collection reliability.

healthSignals.label: Healthy
healthSignals.explanation: 5506 days of cash on hand exceeds the 90-day benchmark, providing a strong liquidity cushion.
healthSignals.watchItem: Maintain this buffer to weather seasonal revenue dips or unexpected expenses.

healthSignals.label: Healthy
healthSignals.explanation: 1511.6 months of reserves by Year 5 — a strong buffer for unexpected costs.
healthSignals.watchItem: Continue growing reserves toward 6 months for best-in-class financial health.

criteria.name: Leader Compensation
criteria.detail: School leader draws a salary. This is a fundamental requirement for sustainable operations.

criteria.name: Philanthropy Dependency
criteria.threshold: ≤25% in every year, decreasing over time
criteria.detail: Fundraising is healthy and encouraged. But your school must be able to keep the lights on without it. At full enrollment, your budget should balance on earned revenue alone. Grants should fund growth and mission extras, not basic operations.

criteria.name: Personnel Cost Ratio
criteria.detail: Personnel costs are well within the target range, leaving healthy room for facilities, curriculum, and operations.

criteria.name: Facility Cost Ratio
criteria.detail: Facility costs are within range but above the ideal 20% target. Consider using SchoolStack Space (space.schoolstack.ai) to evaluate alternative properties.

criteria.name: Debt Service Coverage
criteria.detail: No debt in your model, so DSCR is not applicable. This criterion only applies when you include a loan.

criteria.name: Net Margin Trajectory
criteria.detail: Net margin trajectory is healthy, reaching break-even by Year 3 and growing to a sustainable level.

criteria.name: Minimum Enrollment
criteria.detail: Year 1 enrollment of 90 students meets the minimum viability threshold.

criteria.name: Revenue Per Pupil
criteria.detail: Revenue per pupil of $1,080,756 meets the minimum threshold for sustainable operations.

lendingLabAssessment.summary: Your model meets all Lending Lab criteria. 1 area could be strengthened but won't prevent an application.
